enrichment.short_description: Chegg is a publicly traded (NYSE: CHGG) education technology company founded in 2005 that sells textbook rentals, homework help, expert-answered questions, and study tools to college students. Once valued at over 14 billion dollars, Chegg has suffered one of the most severe, well-documented business collapses in edtech history since 2023, as ChatGPT and other generative AI tools eroded its subscriber base, driving a stock price crash of roughly 99 percent and workforce cuts of nearly half its staff.
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 1,840 employees as of December 2025, down from about 3,270 in 2023 following two rounds of layoffs
enrichment.seo_title: Chegg Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and the AI Disruption Story
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chegg offers textbook rentals, homework help, and study tools for students. See current pricing, features, and how ChatGPT and AI upended its business.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chegg is an American education technology company, publicly traded on the NYSE under the ticker CHGG, that has sold textbook rentals, step-by-step homework solutions, expert-answered questions, writing help, and online tutoring to college students for nearly two decades. Its roots trace to Cheggpost, a Craigslist-style board for Iowa State University students launched in October 2000 by Josh Carlson, Mike Seager, and Mark Fiddleke. Aayush Phumbhra and Osman Rashid incorporated the company in 2005, pivoted into textbook rentals modeled on Netflix, and relaunched the service as Chegg in December 2007. Chegg went public in November 2013, raising about 187.5 million dollars, and under longtime CEO Dan Rosensweig grew into a subscription business built around Chegg Study, Chegg Study Pack, Chegg Writing, Chegg Math Solver, and tutoring. This profile deliberately covers Chegg's current, widely reported business decline rather than glossing over it, because it materially defines the company today. ChatGPT's launch in November 2022 hit Chegg's core value proposition directly: free AI tools could answer many of the same homework questions Chegg charged a monthly subscription for. Chegg's own CEO acknowledged the impact in a May 2023 warning, and the company has since lost more than 500,000 subscribers. Chegg's stock has fallen roughly 99 percent from its 2021 peak, wiping out tens of billions in market value. The company has also sued Google, arguing that AI Overviews in search results have diverted traffic and revenue away from publishers like Chegg. Layoffs followed in two waves during 2025: about 22 percent of staff in May and a further 45 percent of the remaining workforce in October, cutting total headcount to roughly 1,840 by year-end, down from about 3,270 in 2023. Dan Rosensweig returned as President and CEO in October 2025 as part of the restructuring. Rather than sell the company, Chegg's board explored a sale through advisor Goldman Sachs in 2025 but ultimately decided in October 2025 to remain a standalone public company, betting instead on a strategic pivot toward business-to-business skilling: professional language learning, workplace-readiness training, and AI-skills courses aimed at enterprise learning and development budgets rather than individual students. That segment was expected to generate roughly 70 million dollars in 2025 revenue with targeted double-digit growth in 2026. In June 2026, Chegg regained compliance with the NYSE's minimum share price listing standard, leading its board to shelve a previously considered reverse stock split. For readers evaluating Chegg today, the product still functions largely as it always has, textbook rentals, homework help, and study tools, priced around 9.95 to 19.95 dollars per month depending on the plan, but the company's near-term financial trajectory, subscriber trends, and even its long-term independence remain genuinely uncertain given the depth of the AI-driven disruption to its core market.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chegg Offers Students
paragraphs: Chegg's product suite centers on lowering the cost and friction of getting through a college course. Its original business, physical and digital textbook rentals, let students pay a fraction of a textbook's retail price for a semester of access. Around that core, Chegg built Chegg Study, a library of step-by-step solutions to textbook problems, and Expert-answered Q and A, where students can submit a question and get a written answer from a subject-matter expert.

Additional tools rounded out the subscription: Chegg Writing checks papers for plagiarism and grammar issues, Chegg Math Solver walks through equations step by step, and Chegg's online tutoring connects students with live tutors. Chegg Study Pack bundles most of these into a single monthly or annual subscription, which has historically been positioned as cheaper than paying for a semester of textbooks outright.

long_form_blocks.heading: The AI Disruption: Chegg's Stock Collapse and Layoffs
paragraphs: Chegg's business model depended on students being willing to pay a recurring subscription for help that, starting in late 2022, became available for free from ChatGPT and similar generative AI tools. Chegg's own leadership flagged the impact publicly in May 2023, and the company has documented a loss of more than 500,000 subscribers since ChatGPT's launch. The market reaction has been brutal: Chegg's stock has fallen roughly 99 percent from its 2021 peak, when the company was valued at more than 14 billion dollars.

The financial pressure translated directly into headcount reductions. Chegg cut about 22 percent of its staff and closed its US and Canada offices in May 2025, then eliminated a further 45 percent of its remaining workforce in October 2025, a restructuring that brought total 2025 workforce reductions to roughly 56 percent of what the company employed at the start of the year. Dan Rosensweig, who had stepped back to Executive Chairman, returned as President and CEO to lead the restructuring. Compounding the pressure, Chegg has sued Google, alleging that AI-generated summaries in search results (AI Overviews) have further reduced the organic search traffic that once drove students to Chegg's website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Chegg's Pivot to Skills and Where It Stands Today
paragraphs: Rather than sell itself, Chegg's board decided in October 2025 to remain a standalone public company after a strategic review conducted with Goldman Sachs as advisor, including proposals to take the company private. Instead, Chegg is repositioning around a business-to-business skilling strategy, building on existing units in professional language learning, workplace-readiness training, and AI-skills courses aimed at enterprise learning budgets rather than individual student subscriptions. That segment was projected to generate around 70 million dollars in revenue in 2025 with double-digit growth targeted for 2026.

In June 2026, Chegg announced it had regained compliance with the NYSE's minimum share price continued-listing standard, prompting the board to shelve a previously discussed reverse stock split. The consumer-facing textbook rental and homework-help products remain live and priced roughly as before, but Chegg's broader financial outlook, subscriber trajectory, and the ultimate success of its enterprise pivot remain open questions given how directly generative AI continues to compete with its original value proposition.

faqs.question: Is Chegg still in business?
faqs.answer: Yes. As of mid-2026, Chegg remains a standalone public company trading on the NYSE and regained compliance with the exchange's minimum share price standard in June 2026, though it has undergone major restructuring since 2023.

faqs.question: What happened to Chegg's stock price?
faqs.answer: Chegg's stock has fallen roughly 99 percent from its 2021 peak, a decline widely attributed to competition from ChatGPT and other free generative AI tools that reduced demand for Chegg's paid homework-help subscriptions.

faqs.question: Did Chegg get acquired?
faqs.answer: No. Chegg's board explored a potential sale in 2025 with Goldman Sachs as advisor, including proposals to go private, but decided in October 2025 to remain a standalone public company.

faqs.question: How much does Chegg cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing has varied by plan, with Chegg Study historically around 15.95 dollars per month, Chegg Study Pack around 19.95 dollars per month (or about 9.95 dollars per month on an annual plan), and Chegg Writing around 9.95 dollars per month.

faqs.question: Why is Chegg struggling?
faqs.answer: Free generative AI tools like ChatGPT can answer many of the same homework and study questions Chegg historically charged a subscription for, which the company says has directly driven subscriber losses since late 2022.

faqs.question: Is Chegg the same company that does homework help and textbook rentals?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are product lines within the same company, alongside writing help, math solving, tutoring, and newer enterprise skills courses.

faqs.question: What is Chegg's new strategy?
faqs.answer: Chegg is pivoting toward a business-to-business skilling model, offering professional language learning, workplace-readiness training, and AI-skills courses aimed at enterprise learning and development budgets.

faqs.question: Who founded Chegg?
faqs.answer: Chegg traces back to Cheggpost, launched in 2000 by Josh Carlson, Mike Seager, and Mark Fiddleke; the company was incorporated in 2005 by Aayush Phumbhra and Osman Rashid and relaunched as Chegg in December 2007.

enrichment.short_description: Chemtool is a free, open-source 2D chemical structure editor for Linux and other X11-based Unix-like systems, built with the GTK+ toolkit. Originally written by German chemistry student Thomas Volk and later maintained by other contributors, it lets chemists, educators, and students sketch organic molecules, bond diagrams, and reaction schemes, then export them to formats such as PNG, SVG, EPS, XFig, and MDL molfile. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License and remains available through most major Linux distribution package repositories, though active development has slowed considerably in recent years.
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable, a volunteer-maintained open-source project rather than a company
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable, an open-source project originally developed in Germany
enrichment.seo_title: Chemtool Review: Free Open-Source Chemical Structure Editor for Linux
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chemtool is a free, open-source GTK+ tool for drawing 2D chemical structures on Linux. See features, export formats, and how it compares to modern alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chemtool is a small, free, open-source program for drawing two-dimensional chemical structures on Linux and other X11-based Unix-like operating systems. It was originally written by Thomas Volk, at the time a chemistry and biology student at the University of Ulm in Germany, and was later documented in the German periodical LinuxMagazin. Development subsequently continued under other maintainers, with the project's long-standing home page hosted at the University of Freiburg. Chemtool has been packaged in most major Linux distributions for many years, including through standard repositories like apt and dnf, which is how most current users encounter it rather than building it from source. Note that the exact year of Chemtool's first release could not be independently verified from current sources, so this profile avoids stating a specific founding year rather than guessing one. Functionally, Chemtool provides a focused, no-frills canvas for sketching organic molecules. It supports square and hexagonal backdrop grids to keep bond angles consistent, multiple zoom levels, seven colors, two text fonts plus chemical symbols, and essentially unlimited undo and redo. Its bond-drawing toolkit is unusually thorough for such a lightweight program, covering wedge-shaped bonds, dashed wedge bonds, double and centered double bonds, triple and quadruple bonds, wavy lines, half arrows, and wide bonds, enough to represent most standard organic chemistry notation. Structures can be exported to X bitmap, PNG, PicTeX, XFig, SVG, SXD, MDL molfile, or EPS formats, which makes it easy to drop a diagram into a LaTeX document, a presentation, or a publication. Chemtool can also import files in the Protein Data Bank (PDB) format and the MDL molfile format used by many other cheminformatics tools, which gives it some interoperability with larger chemistry software ecosystems despite its small footprint. A companion command-line helper, cht, calculates the sum formula and exact molecular weight of a Chemtool drawing or a marked fragment of one, a convenience also accessible from within the main application. Because Chemtool is released under the GNU General Public License, it costs nothing to use, modify, or redistribute, and there is no signup, account, or subscription involved. That said, prospective users should understand Chemtool's real limitations before choosing it over more modern tools. It is Linux and X11 only, with no native Windows, macOS, or web-based version, so it is not a fit for users on other platforms without running Linux in a virtual machine or via WSL. Its interface, while functional, looks dated next to actively developed commercial tools like ChemDraw or free web-based editors like Ketcher and MarvinSketch, and it has no cloud sync, collaboration features, version history, or 3D visualization. For a Linux user, student, or educator who just needs to quickly sketch a molecule and export it as a clean vector image, Chemtool remains a lightweight, dependable, and completely free option, best understood today as a stable legacy utility rather than an actively evolving product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chemtool Does
paragraphs: Chemtool is a purpose-built drawing tool for organic chemistry notation, not a general vector graphics editor repurposed for chemistry. Its canvas supports square and hexagonal alignment grids, multiple zoom levels, and a wide set of chemistry-specific bond types, including wedge, dashed wedge, double, triple, quadruple, wavy, and wide bonds, which together cover most of what a student or researcher needs to represent standard organic structures accurately.

Once a structure is drawn, Chemtool can export it to a range of formats, including PNG, SVG, EPS, XFig, PicTeX, and X bitmap, as well as import and export the MDL molfile format and import Protein Data Bank (PDB) files. A bundled helper utility, cht, calculates the molecular formula and exact molecular weight of a drawing or a selected fragment, a small but genuinely useful feature for coursework and quick reference.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Still Uses Chemtool and How It Compares
paragraphs: Because Chemtool is packaged directly in the repositories of most major Linux distributions, many users encounter it simply by searching their distro's package manager for a chemical structure editor rather than actively seeking it out online. Its GPL license and minimal system requirements have kept it alive and installable for years even as development activity around the core project has slowed.

Compared with actively maintained modern alternatives, such as the commercial ChemDraw, or free web-based tools like Ketcher and MarvinSketch, Chemtool's interface and feature set look dated: there is no cloud collaboration, no 3D structure visualization, and no reaction-mechanism tooling. What it does offer is a lightweight, fast, completely free, and Linux-native way to produce clean 2D structure diagrams, which is exactly the niche it continues to fill for students, educators, and hobbyists working on Linux.

faqs.question: Is Chemtool free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chemtool is free and open source, released under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: What platforms does Chemtool run on?
faqs.answer: Chemtool runs on Linux and other X11-based Unix-like systems; it does not have a native Windows, macOS, or web version.

faqs.question: Who created Chemtool?
faqs.answer: Chemtool was originally written by Thomas Volk, a German chemistry and biology student, and has since been maintained by other contributors.

faqs.question: What file formats can Chemtool export to?
faqs.answer: Chemtool can export to PNG, SVG, EPS, XFig, PicTeX, X bitmap, and the MDL molfile format.

faqs.question: Can Chemtool calculate molecular weight?
faqs.answer: Yes, via its built-in cht helper utility, which computes the sum formula and exact molecular weight of a structure or fragment.

faqs.question: Is Chemtool still actively developed?
faqs.answer: Development has slowed significantly in recent years; it functions today as a stable, installable legacy tool rather than a frequently updated product.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Chemtool?
faqs.answer: Commercial and web-based alternatives include ChemDraw, Ketcher, MarvinSketch, and BKChem.

faqs.question: Do I need to build Chemtool from source?
faqs.answer: Usually not. Chemtool is packaged in the repositories of most major Linux distributions and can typically be installed directly through the package manager.

enrichment.short_description: CherryTree is a free, open-source hierarchical note-taking application created by developer Giuseppe Penone that organizes notes as a tree of nodes, each of which can hold rich text, syntax-highlighted code, images, tables, and embedded files. Released under the GPLv3 license and available for Windows, macOS, and Linux, it stores entire notebooks in a single portable XML or SQLite file, optionally with document-level password protection, which has made it a long-running favorite for offline personal knowledge bases, technical documentation, and IT run-books.
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable, an open-source project maintained by an individual developer and community contributors
enrichment.seo_title: CherryTree Review: Free Hierarchical Note-Taking App for Windows, Mac, and Linux
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CherryTree is a free, open-source note-taking app with a tree-based node structure, rich text, syntax highlighting, and password protection.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CherryTree is a free, open-source, hierarchical note-taking application created by developer Giuseppe Penone, first released around 2009, with the codebase later reworked in C++ and GTK by Penone along with contributor Evgenii Gurianov. Rather than organizing notes as a flat list of documents, CherryTree structures every notebook as a tree: each entry is a node that can have any number of child nodes, letting users nest related notes the way folders nest inside other folders. This structure makes CherryTree particularly well suited to information that naturally has sub-topics, such as project documentation, server configuration notes, or a personal knowledge base organized by subject area. Within each node, CherryTree supports genuinely rich formatting rather than plain text alone: bold, italic, underline, strikethrough, text and background colors, hyperlinks, bulleted and numbered lists, tables, embedded images, and file attachments. For technical users, one of CherryTree's standout features is syntax-highlighted code boxes covering dozens of programming and markup languages, which makes it a popular choice for developers and system administrators who want to keep command references, configuration snippets, and troubleshooting notes alongside their prose documentation. It also supports LaTeX syntax for rendering mathematical notation, useful for students and researchers. A defining architectural choice is how CherryTree stores data: an entire notebook, however large, is saved as a single XML or SQLite file by default (a multi-file, directory-based storage option is also available). This makes notebooks trivially easy to back up, move between machines, or carry on a USB drive, and it avoids any dependency on a particular cloud service. Security-conscious users can apply a password to a notebook, which encrypts the entire document; note that this protection applies at the whole-document level rather than to individual nodes. CherryTree is cross-platform, running natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and its search functionality can scan an entire note tree or be scoped to a specific branch, which matters once a notebook grows into the hundreds of nodes. What CherryTree does not offer is worth being equally clear about: there is no official mobile app, and it has no built-in real-time cloud sync or multi-user collaboration, so teams or users wanting automatic cross-device sync typically place the storage file inside a service like Dropbox or Nextcloud and rely on that service's own syncing. The interface, while functional and consistent, also looks more utilitarian than newer note-taking apps like Notion or Obsidian, and the tree-based mental model takes some adjustment for users used to flat document lists or a graph-based linking approach. CherryTree remains actively maintained on GitHub by Penone and community contributors, and it is entirely free to use, with the project accepting optional donations rather than charging for the software itself. For anyone who wants a fast, offline-first, deeply nested note-taking tool without any subscription or cloud lock-in, CherryTree continues to be one of the most capable free options available.

long_form_blocks.heading: How CherryTree Organizes Notes
paragraphs: CherryTree's core idea is the tree of nodes: every note is a node, and any node can have an unlimited number of child nodes, letting users build deeply nested hierarchies that mirror how a topic naturally breaks down into sub-topics. This is a meaningfully different model from flat note lists or tag-based systems, and it tends to appeal most to users documenting structured, hierarchical information such as a codebase, a server fleet, or a course syllabus.

Inside each node, CherryTree offers genuinely rich formatting: standard text styling, hyperlinks, bulleted and numbered lists, embedded images and tables, LaTeX for mathematical notation, and syntax-highlighted code boxes covering a long list of programming and markup languages. That code-highlighting support in particular has made CherryTree popular among developers and system administrators who want technical notes, snippets, and prose documentation living side by side in the same tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Storage, Security, and Cross-Platform Support
paragraphs: By default, CherryTree stores an entire notebook, no matter how large, as a single XML or SQLite file, with a multi-file directory option also available for users who prefer it. This single-file design makes backup and manual portability simple: copying, versioning, or carrying a notebook on removable media is as easy as handling one file. Users who want extra security can apply a password to a notebook, which encrypts the whole document rather than individual notes or branches.

CherryTree runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux with a consistent feature set across all three, and it can export notes to other formats when content needs to leave the app. What it does not include is a built-in cloud sync service or an official mobile app, so users wanting automatic cross-device access commonly store the CherryTree file inside a third-party sync folder such as Dropbox, Google Drive, or Nextcloud and let that service handle the syncing.

enrichment.short_description: Chili Piper is a B2B revenue-acceleration platform that closes the gap between an inbound lead filling out a form and getting a qualified meeting on a sales rep's calendar. Founded in 2016 by husband-and-wife team Alina and Nicolas Vandenberghe and headquartered in New York City, it combines instant meeting booking, rules-based lead routing, and internal handoff scheduling to help revenue teams cut speed-to-lead and lift inbound conversion rates, with customers reported to include Intuit, Twilio, Forrester, Spotify, and Gong.

enrichment.company_size: Mid-size private company (exact headcount not fully disclosed publicly; reported to serve roughly 2,000 customers)

enrichment.seo_title: Chili Piper Review: Meeting Scheduling and Lead Routing Software for Sales Teams
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chili Piper automates inbound lead routing and meeting scheduling for B2B sales teams. See features, pricing, pros and cons, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chili Piper is a revenue-acceleration platform built to solve two of the most common leak points in a B2B inbound sales funnel: the delay between a lead submitting a form and a rep following up (speed-to-lead), and the friction of manually finding a mutual meeting time afterward. The company was founded in 2016 by Alina and Nicolas Vandenberghe and is headquartered in New York City. It raised an early Series A of 18 million dollars led by Base10 Partners, with participation from Google's AI-focused venture fund Gradient Ventures and Flashpoint Venture Capital, and has reportedly raised around 58 million dollars in total funding across multiple rounds. Chili Piper's product suite is organized around the moments where inbound leads are won or lost. Concierge, its instant-booking product, embeds a live calendar directly on a website's thank-you page after a form submission, so a prospect can book time with the correct rep immediately instead of waiting for a follow-up email. Distro is Chili Piper's lead-routing engine, assigning inbound leads to the right rep automatically based on rules like territory, existing account ownership, company size, or round-robin distribution, replacing the manual assignment rules many teams previously built and maintained in Salesforce. Handoff extends that same routing and scheduling logic to internal handoffs, automating the process of passing a prospect from an SDR to an account executive, or from sales to onboarding and customer success, without back-and-forth emails. Underpinning all three products is deep integration with Salesforce and HubSpot, letting Chili Piper read and write lead, contact, and opportunity records in near real time, along with calendar integrations for Google Calendar and Outlook. The company acquired scheduling startup KosmoTime in 2022 and underwent a brand refresh in 2024. Chili Piper reports serving roughly 2,000 customers, with publicly cited logos including Intuit, Twilio, Forrester, Spotify, and Gong, positioning it firmly as a tool for established B2B sales and revenue operations organizations rather than early-stage startups. Pricing is not published in detail on Chili Piper's website; the company instead sells through custom quotes, and third-party pricing trackers commonly report plans starting around 15,000 dollars per year, scaling with the number of seats and which products (Concierge, Distro, Handoff) a team adopts. That pricing model, combined with the complexity of configuring routing rules across territories, ownership models, and round-robin logic, generally means Chili Piper is adopted by revenue operations teams at mid-market and enterprise companies with the resources to configure and maintain it, rather than by small businesses evaluating their first scheduling tool. For teams that have outgrown basic calendar-link scheduling and are losing pipeline to slow lead response times or messy manual routing rules, Chili Piper remains one of the more established, purpose-built platforms addressing that specific problem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chili Piper Solves for Revenue Teams
paragraphs: Chili Piper was built around a well-documented sales problem: the longer it takes a rep to respond to and schedule time with an inbound lead, the lower the odds that lead ever converts. Generic contact forms and manual routing rules built in a CRM tend to introduce exactly that delay, especially as a company scales past a handful of reps and territories.

The platform addresses this with three connected products. Concierge lets a prospect book a meeting with the right rep the moment they submit a form, rather than waiting for a follow-up email. Distro automatically routes inbound leads to the correct rep based on territory, account ownership, company size, or round-robin logic. Handoff extends that same automation to internal transitions, such as an SDR passing a qualified prospect to an account executive, or sales handing a closed deal to onboarding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Origins, Funding, and Customer Base
paragraphs: Chili Piper was founded in 2016 by husband-and-wife team Alina and Nicolas Vandenberghe and is headquartered in New York City. The company raised an 18 million dollar Series A led by Base10 Partners, with Google's Gradient Ventures and Flashpoint Venture Capital also participating, and has reportedly raised roughly 58 million dollars in total across its funding history. Chili Piper acquired scheduling startup KosmoTime in 2022 and completed a brand refresh in partnership with Veza Digital in 2024.

The company reports serving roughly 2,000 customers, with publicly referenced logos including Intuit, Twilio, Forrester, Spotify, and Gong. That customer profile reflects Chili Piper's positioning: a tool for revenue operations and sales leadership at companies with enough inbound volume and rep headcount to justify automated routing and scheduling infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Who Chili Piper Is Built For
paragraphs: Chili Piper does not publish detailed self-serve pricing; instead, prospective customers work with a sales team to get a custom quote, typically scaled by number of seats and which of the three core products (Concierge, Distro, Handoff) are included. Third-party sources commonly report plans starting around 15,000 dollars per year, which places Chili Piper firmly in enterprise software territory rather than the self-serve SaaS tier.

Given both the pricing model and the configuration effort required to set up meaningful routing rules across territories and ownership models, Chili Piper is generally best suited to revenue operations teams at mid-market and enterprise B2B companies with dedicated staff to own the implementation, rather than early-stage startups looking for a lightweight scheduling link.

Chime offers fee-free mobile banking, including a checking account, savings account, and a secured Credit Builder card, delivered through partner banks Bancorp Bank and Stride Bank rather than as a chartered bank itself. Founded around 2012-2013 by Chris Britt and Ryan King and headquartered in San Francisco, Chime built its business serving low- and middle-income, often underbanked, Americans, and went public on the Nasdaq in June 2025 under the ticker CHME in one of the largest US neobank IPOs to date.

enrichment.seo_title: Chime Review 2026: Fees, Features, and How the Neobank Works
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chime is a fee-free US mobile banking app with no monthly fees, early paycheck access, and a Credit Builder card. See features, pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: The name Chime is shared by at least three unrelated products, so it is worth being explicit about which one this profile covers. This is Chime, the US consumer financial technology company and mobile banking app, most often referred to as a neobank. It is not Amazon Chime, Amazon's separate video conferencing and business communications service, and it is not the real estate CRM once marketed as Chime, which rebranded to Lofty in 2023 and has no relationship to the financial company. Chime the neobank was founded around 2012 to 2013 by Chris Britt and Ryan King and is headquartered in San Francisco. Chime is not itself a chartered bank; its checking and savings accounts are issued by partner banks, The Bancorp Bank and Stride Bank, N.A., with deposits FDIC-insured through those partners. This structure is common among neobanks and lets Chime focus on the mobile app, product design, and customer experience while partner banks handle regulated deposit-taking. Chime's core product is a fee-free Checking Account with no monthly maintenance fees, no minimum balance requirements, and no typical overdraft fees, paired with features aimed squarely at customers living paycheck to paycheck: direct deposit that can post up to two days early, and SpotMe, a fee-free overdraft feature that covers small negative balances up to a limit that can grow with account history. Chime also offers a High-Yield Savings Account and a Credit Builder secured Visa card, which reports payment activity to credit bureaus and carries no annual fee or interest charge, making it a relatively low-risk way for customers to establish or repair a credit history. Because Chime does not charge the fees many traditional banks rely on, its revenue comes primarily from interchange fees paid by merchants when customers use their Chime debit or credit builder cards. Chime scaled significantly on this model: it reported roughly 1.7 billion dollars in revenue in 2024, dramatically narrowed its annual loss to about 25 million dollars, and turned profitable in the first quarter of 2025, with more than 518 million dollars in that single quarter's revenue. That growth culminated in Chime's initial public offering on June 12, 2025, when it began trading on the Nasdaq under the ticker CHME, raising roughly 864 million dollars and valuing the company at figures reported between about 11.2 and 18.4 billion dollars depending on the measure used, with shares reportedly popping as much as 59 percent above the offering price on their debut. That IPO valuation sits well below Chime's reported 2021 private funding peak of around 25 billion dollars, reflecting the broader fintech valuation reset since then, but it was still widely covered as a milestone IPO that helped restart fintech public listings after a slow stretch for the sector. For consumers evaluating Chime today, it functions as a genuinely fee-light alternative to a traditional bank account, particularly useful for people who want to avoid overdraft and minimum-balance charges, though it lacks physical branches and, as with any partner-bank fintech, ultimately depends on Bancorp Bank and Stride Bank for the underlying regulated banking infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Which Chime Is This? Disambiguating a Crowded Name
paragraphs: Anyone researching Chime should be aware that at least three distinct products have used the name. This profile is specifically about Chime, the US consumer fintech and mobile banking app founded by Chris Britt and Ryan King. It is not Amazon Chime, which is Amazon's own video conferencing and business communications product, an entirely separate offering from an entirely separate company. It is also not the real estate CRM formerly branded as Chime, which rebranded to Lofty in 2023 and serves real estate agents and brokerages, again with no connection to the financial company described here.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Chime's Banking App Works
paragraphs: Chime is not a chartered bank; it is a financial technology company that partners with The Bancorp Bank and Stride Bank, N.A. to offer FDIC-insured checking and savings accounts through its mobile app. Its flagship Checking Account carries no monthly maintenance fees, no minimum balance requirements, and no typical overdraft charges, positioning it as an alternative to fee-heavy traditional bank accounts for people living paycheck to paycheck.

Signature features build on that fee-free foundation: direct deposit that can post up to two days early, SpotMe fee-free overdraft coverage up to a limit that can grow over time, a High-Yield Savings Account with automatic savings tools, and a Credit Builder secured Visa card that reports to credit bureaus without charging interest or an annual fee. Because Chime does not rely on account fees, its revenue instead comes primarily from card interchange fees paid by merchants when customers spend.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Startup to Nasdaq: Chime's Growth and 2025 IPO
paragraphs: Chime grew from a 2012-2013 startup into the largest neobank serving underbanked US consumers, reporting around 8.6 million active members and roughly 1.7 billion dollars in 2024 revenue, alongside a dramatically narrowed annual loss and a return to profitability in early 2025. That growth set up its initial public offering on June 12, 2025, when Chime began trading on the Nasdaq under ticker CHME, raising about 864 million dollars in the offering.

Reported valuations from the IPO ranged from roughly 11.2 billion to 18.4 billion dollars depending on the measure used, notably below Chime's reported 2021 private valuation peak of around 25 billion dollars, reflecting the broader reset in fintech valuations since then. Even so, shares reportedly jumped as much as 59 percent above the offering price on their debut, and the listing was widely framed as a milestone for reopening the fintech IPO market after several quiet years.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: The name Chisel is shared by two unrelated software products, so it is worth clarifying upfront which one this profile covers. This is Chisel.app, also known as Chisel Labs, a product management and roadmapping platform, and it is not the same as the open-source project also called chisel, a Go-based command-line tool for tunneling TCP and UDP traffic over HTTP, which serves a completely different, network-engineering audience. Chisel Labs was founded in 2020 and built its product around a specific complaint from product managers: that roadmapping, customer feedback collection, and cross-functional team alignment usually lived in three or more disconnected tools, spreadsheets, and slide decks. Chisel's platform combined agile, customizable roadmaps (with kanban, release, and timeline views) with a feature called Team Radar, designed to surface where stakeholders across an organization agreed or disagreed on priorities so disagreements could be resolved proactively rather than discovered late. On the feedback side, an IdeaBox let teams capture, curate, and prioritize feature ideas and requests from multiple sources, while a Customer Portal and built-in surveys gave customers a dedicated space to submit feedback and track progress on requests they cared about. More recently, Chisel added an AI PM agent capable of drafting product requirement documents (PRDs) and release notes, summarizing user research, triaging incoming feedback, and flagging anomalies in a roadmap automatically, aiming to save product managers meaningful hours each week on repetitive documentation work. The platform integrated with a broad set of tools common in product organizations, including Jira, Azure DevOps, Salesforce, Zendesk, Intercom, GitHub, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Confluence, and Microsoft Teams, plus thousands more through Zapier. Historically, Chisel's pricing followed a free-forever model for core roadmapping and team alignment features, with paid add-ons, publicly reported at various points around 79 dollars per month per account for unlimited stakeholders and Jira integration, and around 49 dollars per month for customer surveys and additional integrations, though buyers should confirm current terms directly rather than assume these historical figures still apply. The most consequential recent development is that Pendo, a software experience management platform, acquired Chisel Labs on February 3, 2026, in what Pendo described as its fourth acquisition in 18 months and third acquisition of an AI-native startup, explicitly aimed at expanding Pendo's agentic AI capabilities for product teams. As part of the deal, Chisel's engineering team based in Pune, India, is becoming the foundation of Pendo's tenth global office, with plans to scale that team to 50 engineers by the end of 2026. For anyone researching Chisel today, the practical implication is that it is no longer an independently operating startup: prospective and existing customers should check directly with Pendo for current product availability, packaging, and pricing rather than relying solely on Chisel's pre-acquisition marketing, since the product's future roadmap and go-to-market are now controlled by its acquirer.

The two products share a name and nothing else, so anyone landing on generic search results for chisel should confirm which one they are looking at.

paragraphs: Chisel positioned itself as a single home for the core work of product management: agile, customizable roadmaps across kanban, release, and timeline views; a Team Radar feature meant to surface where cross-functional stakeholders agreed or disagreed on priorities; an IdeaBox for capturing and organizing feature requests from multiple sources; and a Customer Portal with built-in surveys for structured feedback collection.

Its more recent differentiator was an AI PM agent that could draft product requirement documents and release notes, summarize user research, triage incoming feedback, and flag anomalies in a roadmap automatically. The platform integrated broadly with tools already common in product organizations, including Jira, Azure DevOps, Salesforce, Zendesk, Intercom, GitHub, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Confluence, and Microsoft Teams, with thousands of additional connections available through Zapier.

paragraphs: On February 3, 2026, Pendo, a software experience management platform, acquired Chisel Labs, describing it as Pendo's fourth acquisition in 18 months and third acquisition of an AI-native startup, with the explicit goal of expanding Pendo's own agentic AI capabilities for product teams. Chisel's engineering team based in Pune, India, is becoming the foundation of Pendo's new India office, with plans to scale to 50 engineers by the end of 2026.

For readers evaluating Chisel today, this means it is no longer an independent company setting its own roadmap and pricing. enrichment.seo_title: Chrome Remote Desktop Review: Google's Free Remote Access Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chrome Remote Desktop lets you access computers remotely for free via your browser or mobile app. See features, limitations, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chrome Remote Desktop is Google's free remote-access product, originally launched around 2011 as a beta Chrome browser extension and since evolved into a standalone web app plus dedicated Android and iOS apps as Google moved away from packaged Chrome apps. The service lets a user control another computer's screen and input remotely, authenticated through a Google account and connected using WebRTC, the same real-time communication technology behind many browser-based video and screen-sharing tools. Chrome Remote Desktop supports two distinct usage modes. The first, sometimes called My Computers or Remote Access, lets a person set up long-term access to their own machines, useful for reaching a home desktop from a work laptop or accessing a work computer while traveling. The second, Remote Support, generates a single-use access code that a helper can use to connect to someone else's computer for one session, view and control it to fix a problem, and then disconnect, after which the code expires. During an active session, Chrome Remote Desktop also supports transferring files by upload or download and viewing across multiple monitors on the remote machine. Because it runs through Chrome or Chromium and Google's infrastructure rather than requiring separate licensed desktop software, setup is generally simple: install the host component on the machine to be accessed, sign in with a Google account, and connect from any other supported device. The product is completely free, with no subscription tiers, feature paywalls, or usage limits published, which sets it apart from most competing remote-access tools. That said, Chrome Remote Desktop is deliberately a lightweight product rather than an enterprise remote-support platform, and understanding its limitations matters for choosing the right tool. It does not support multiple simultaneous connections to the same device, which rules it out for use cases like a support team needing shared visibility into one machine at once. It has no built-in chat or messaging during a session, no unattended device fleet management or admin dashboard for IT teams managing many machines, and no session recording, features that dedicated paid remote-support tools such as TeamViewer, AnyDesk, or Splashtop are built around. It also periodically re-prompts the person sharing their screen to confirm they still want the session active, roughly every 30 minutes in Remote Support sessions, which is a helpful safeguard for casual users but can interrupt long unattended technical work. In practice, Chrome Remote Desktop remains best suited to individuals accessing their own devices, casual one-off tech support between family members or friends, and small teams or hobbyists who need occasional remote access without paying for or deploying enterprise software, while organizations needing fleet management, compliance logging, or simultaneous multi-user access should look toward paid alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Chrome Remote Desktop Works
paragraphs: Chrome Remote Desktop offers two modes built for different situations. My Computers, or Remote Access, is for reaching your own devices, for example connecting to a home desktop from a work laptop, and is set up once with a PIN for repeated future access. Remote Support instead generates a single-use access code, ideal for one-off help sessions, letting a helper connect once, fix an issue, and disconnect, after which the code expires and cannot be reused.

Setup involves installing a small host component on the machine being accessed and signing in with a Google account, after which connections can be made from a browser, the dedicated Chrome Remote Desktop web app, or the Android and iOS mobile apps. Sessions run over WebRTC, giving encrypted, real-time connections, and support file transfer and multi-monitor viewing, all without requiring a licensed third-party remote-desktop client.

long_form_blocks.heading: What It's Good For and Where It Falls Short
paragraphs: Chrome Remote Desktop's biggest strengths are its price and simplicity: it is entirely free, works across essentially every major desktop and mobile platform, and can typically be set up by a non-technical user in a few minutes using nothing but a Google account and a browser. That makes it a strong default choice for individuals and casual, informal tech support scenarios.

Its limitations become apparent for anyone managing remote access at scale. It supports only one active connection per device at a time, has no built-in chat, and lacks the unattended fleet management, admin dashboards, and session recording that paid, enterprise-oriented tools like TeamViewer, AnyDesk, and Splashtop are designed around. Periodic prompts to confirm continued screen sharing, while good for privacy on casual sessions, can also interrupt longer unattended technical work. Teams needing centralized device management or compliance features should generally look to a paid alternative instead.

enrichment.short_description: Churnkey is a subscription retention platform that helps SaaS companies reduce voluntary and involuntary churn through no-code cancel flows, dunning and payment recovery, A/B testing, and AI-generated retention offers. Founded in 2020 and headquartered near Charleston in Mount Pleasant, South Carolina, and backed by investors including TinySeed and CreativeCo Capital, Churnkey plugs into billing providers like Stripe and Chargebee to intercept the cancellation moment with targeted pause, downgrade, or discount offers, reporting churn reductions of up to 54 percent and payment recovery rates as high as 89 percent for customers.

enrichment.seo_title: Churnkey Review: Cancel Flow and Churn Reduction Software for SaaS
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Churnkey builds no-code cancel flows and payment recovery tools to reduce SaaS churn. See pricing, features, pros and cons, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Churnkey is a subscription retention platform built specifically around the moment a customer tries to cancel, treating that single interaction as a distinct product surface worth designing, testing, and optimizing rather than leaving it to a generic settings page. The company was founded in 2020, is headquartered in Mount Pleasant, South Carolina, near Charleston, and has been backed by investors including startup accelerator TinySeed and CreativeCo Capital, with reported funding of roughly 1.8 million dollars including a 1.5 million dollar growth equity round in April 2024. Churnkey addresses both major categories of subscription churn. Voluntary churn, when a customer actively decides to cancel, is handled through a no-code cancel flow builder that lets non-engineering teams design branching flows with feedback questions, and then present adaptive, AI-personalized offers, such as a plan pause, a downgrade, or a targeted discount, aimed at retaining the customer before they leave entirely. The company reports these cancel flows can reduce active churn by up to 54 percent. Involuntary churn, caused by failed or declined payments, is addressed through Churnkey's dunning and payment recovery tools, which use rules-based retry logic to automatically re-attempt failed charges; the company reports recovering up to 89 percent of failed payments through this system. Layered on top of these core flows are several AI-driven features: an Insights AI module automatically analyzes and categorizes raw cancellation feedback so teams can spot patterns without manually reading every response, a Build with AI feature can generate entire cancel flow steps, copy, and offers from a simple prompt, and automatic AI-powered translation converts cancel flows into multiple languages so international customers get a native-language experience. For mobile-first subscription businesses, Churnkey offers a Hosted Cancel Flow link that can be shared for any platform without engineering work, plus an open-source SDK that can be used as a drop-in component, a headless hook, or a core state machine for teams that want to build a fully custom interface. Churnkey integrates natively with the two most common subscription billing platforms, Stripe and Chargebee, along with Slack and generic webhook-based tooling for alerting and reporting. On the compliance side, all plans include GDPR compliance and SOC 2 certification, which reduces procurement friction for security-conscious buyers. Pricing is tiered primarily by monthly churn volume rather than by seats: the Starter plan costs 250 dollars per month, billed yearly, and is intended for companies with less than 5,000 dollars in monthly churn volume; the Core and Intelligence tiers are reserved for companies with 10,000 dollars or more in monthly churn volume and are sold via custom quote, with Intelligence adding the more advanced AI features like adaptive offers and an account agent; an Enterprise tier adds custom integrations, a dedicated technical account manager, white-label options, and legal support. Churnkey remains a relatively small company, reportedly around 15 employees serving roughly 300 customers, which is worth weighing against its strong reported retention outcomes when evaluating it for a larger or more security-sensitive organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Problem Churnkey Solves
paragraphs: Most SaaS companies lose revenue two ways: customers who actively decide to cancel (voluntary churn), and customers whose payment simply fails and never gets fixed (involuntary churn). Generic, unoptimized cancellation pages tend to make the first problem worse, offering no reason for a wavering customer to stay, while default billing-provider retry logic often leaves the second problem only partially addressed.

Churnkey treats the cancellation moment itself as a product to be designed and iterated on, similar to how a growth team might treat a signup flow. Rather than a single static cancel button, Churnkey's flows collect feedback, present alternatives like a pause or downgrade, and route the customer toward the outcome most likely to retain them, while its payment recovery tools separately work to reduce the churn caused by declined transactions that were never really a cancellation decision at all.

long_form_blocks.heading: Inside Churnkey's Product: Cancel Flows, Recovery, and AI
paragraphs: Churnkey's no-code cancel flow builder lets non-engineering teams design branching flows with feedback questions and offers such as pauses, downgrades, or discounts, and A/B test different versions against each other to see what actually retains customers. Its payment recovery system layers rules-based retry logic on top of a company's existing Stripe or Chargebee billing setup to automatically re-attempt failed charges before they turn into involuntary cancellations.

More recent additions lean on AI: Insights AI automatically analyzes and categorizes raw cancellation feedback into patterns a team can act on, a Build with AI feature can generate flow steps, copy, and offers from a simple prompt, and automatic AI translation localizes cancel flows into multiple languages. For subscription apps distributed through app stores, a Hosted Cancel Flow link or an open-source SDK lets teams add a compliant, on-brand cancellation experience with little or no engineering work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Who Churnkey Is Built For
paragraphs: Churnkey's pricing scales with a company's monthly churn volume rather than simple seat counts. The Starter plan, at 250 dollars per month billed yearly, targets companies with under 5,000 dollars in monthly churn volume and includes cancel flows, payment recovery, and core metrics. Companies with 10,000 dollars or more in monthly churn volume move to the custom-quoted Core or Intelligence tiers, with Intelligence adding adaptive AI offers, an account agent, Feedback AI, and AI-powered translations; an Enterprise tier adds custom integrations, a dedicated technical account manager, and white-label options.

This structure, plus built-in GDPR and SOC 2 compliance, makes Churnkey best suited to SaaS and subscription companies with meaningful, measurable churn volume who want a dedicated tool for the cancellation and payment-recovery moment rather than a general customer-engagement platform repurposed for retention.
